title: Generalized Random Forests Using Fixed-Point Trees

reviews.summary: This paper proposes GRF-FPT, a modification to the Stage I splitting procedure of generalized random forests (GRFs) that replaces the gradient/Jacobian-based pseudo-outcomes with a single fixed-point step using only score evaluations. The key idea is to approximate child-node parameters via one Picard-like update from the parent estimate, thereby avoiding Jacobian estimation and inversion; this yields an efficient CART-ready pseudo-outcome. The authors prove asymptotic equivalence of their splitting criterion to a weighted oracle heterogeneity criterion and argue that, because Stage II is unchanged, the standard GRF consistency and asymptotic normality guarantees apply. Empirically, GRF-FPT attains up to 3.5× training speedup without loss of accuracy across simulated varying-coefficient and heterogeneous treatment effect models, and demonstrates scalability on a real California housing dataset.
strengths: Introduces a simple yet effective fixed-point pseudo-outcome that removes the need for Jacobian estimation/inversion while preserving the spirit of GRF’s heterogeneity-driven splitting.

Establishes an asymptotic link between the proposed fixed-point splitting criterion and an oracle, V-weighted heterogeneity measure, clarifying when and why the approximation should be well aligned with the target.
Presents a further acceleration via a one-step OLS approximation for the parent parameter, maintaining asymptotic equivalence of splits.
Demonstrates substantial and consistent wall-clock speedups that grow with parameter dimension K, with thorough timing breakdowns across multiple n and K.
Shows comparable estimation accuracy (MSE) versus GRF’s gradient-based splitting in multiple simulation settings, plus qualitative real-data results.
Includes stress tests illustrating improved split stability under correlated regressors where Jacobian-based approximations are ill-conditioned.
Clear decomposition of Stage I vs. Stage II, and alignment of the new pseudo-outcomes with standard CART splitting.
Good use of figures to illustrate both instability of gradient-based splits and invariance of fixed-point splitting to step-size scaling.
Addresses a real pain point for practitioners using GRFs at higher target dimension K or under collinearity, reducing both time and numerical instability.
Retains GRF’s inferential guarantees, making the method a drop-in, scalable alternative for heterogeneous effect estimation.
weaknesses: The main theory relies on assumptions (e.g., twice-differentiability of the moment map and Neyman orthogonality) that exclude some canonical GRF applications (e.g., quantile regression with non-smooth scores), despite claims of broad applicability.

Asymptotic equivalence to a V-weighted oracle criterion does not fully characterize finite-sample behavior, especially when θ-coordinates have very different scales or when Vθθ varies rapidly within nodes.
Potential sensitivity to component-wise scaling of ψ (beyond the scalar η); lack of discussion on preconditioning/normalization across θ-coordinates.
Comparisons are limited to GRF-grad; missing baselines such as causal forests with transformed outcomes or model-based recursive partitioning variants that do not require Jacobians.
No evaluation on settings with estimated nuisances (e.g., observational HTE with propensity and outcome models), which are common GRF use cases and stress Neyman-orthogonalization claims.
Limited analysis of accuracy as K grows very large (e.g., 512+) or when X is high-dimensional, and no confidence interval coverage assessments despite asymptotic normality claims.

Some notational typos (e.g., hats and subscripts) and artifacts from PDF extraction obscure a few definitions; the relation between Δ, ΔV, and their approximations could be summarized more succinctly.
The distinction between “gradient-free” and “Jacobian-free” could be made clearer; the method still uses score gradients with respect to data, just not Jacobians in parameter space.
Overlooks Orthogonal Random Forests and other tree-based methods for GMM/Z-estimation that aim to avoid ill-conditioning or provide different splitting surrogates.
Limited discussion on local linear forests and distributional forests as alternative ways to stabilize splits or improve local approximation quality.
reviews.questions:
1. How sensitive is the fixed-point splitting to differences in scale across θ-coordinates or to heterogeneous noise across components? Would a simple diagonal preconditioner (e.g., based on per-coordinate score variance) materially improve finite-sample performance without reintroducing instability?
2. Your theory assumes twice-differentiable moment maps and Neyman orthogonality. Can you clarify which GRF use cases (e.g., quantile regression, instrumental variables) fall outside these assumptions, and whether FPT still behaves well there empirically?
3. In observational HTE, GRF commonly uses orthogonalized scores with estimated nuisances. Did you evaluate GRF-FPT with nuisance estimation (e.g., propensity and outcome models) and, if so, how do training time, stability, and accuracy compare to GRF-grad?
4. Could you provide confidence interval coverage results (or asymptotic variance estimates) for GRF-FPT in simulations to substantiate Theorem 4.4 in practice?
5. The accelerated pseudo-outcomes use a one-step OLS approximation with exact line search. What is the asymptotic and finite-sample trade-off between this approximation and using the full parent OLS, and how does this vary with K and collinearity?
6. Beyond η-scaling invariance, is the ranking of candidate splits invariant to linear rescalings of ψ blocks (e.g., rescaling outcome or regressors)? If not, can you recommend a normalization that preserves efficiency while improving robustness?
7. How does GRF-FPT perform for very large K (e.g., K ≥ 512) and higher-dimensional X, and what are the memory/time bottlenecks in those regimes?
reviews.assessment:
This paper offers a clean, implementable modification to GRF splitting that removes Jacobian estimation and inversion, yielding consistent training speedups and improved numerical stability in the presence of collinearity, without sacrificing accuracy in the tested regimes. The core idea—using a single fixed-point step as a pseudo-outcome—has an appealing simplicity and is supported by an asymptotic equivalence result to a V-weighted oracle criterion and by reusing GRF’s established Stage II guarantees. The contribution is primarily methodological and computational rather than deeply theoretical, but it meaningfully advances the practicality of GRFs for higher-dimensional targets.

The main areas for improvement are: (i) clarifying the scope of the theory (especially relative to non-smooth scores and non-orthogonal settings), (ii) strengthening empirical validation in observational HTE with estimated nuisances and additional baselines, and (iii) discussing scale/preconditioning issues and providing coverage diagnostics. Overall, I view the work as a solid, useful contribution that should be of interest to the ICML community focused on causal/semiparametric methods and scalable tree ensembles. I lean toward acceptance, contingent on addressing the above points in revision.
